Known stale dirs: ddx-bead, ddx-run, ddx-agent, ddx-review, ddx-status, ddx-doctor, ddx-install, ddx-release. Check each of .claude/skills/, .agents/skills/, .ddx/skills/. Do NOT touch third-party skills — only the known ddx-prefixed allowlist.
3. AGENTS.md: merge, do not clobber. Use marker-delimited injection like the existing CLAUDE.md meta-prompt injector (see cli/internal/metaprompt/). Wrap DDx content in:
     <!-- DDX-AGENTS:START -->
     (DDx block)
     <!-- DDX-AGENTS:END -->

Document-Driven Development eXperience

DDx is a toolkit for AI-assisted development that helps you:

• Share templates, prompts, and patterns across projects
• Maintain consistent development practices
• Integrate AI tooling seamlessly
• Contribute improvements back to the community

Get started:
  ddx init          Initialize DDx in your project
  ddx list          See available resources
  ddx doctor        Check installation and diagnose issues

More information:
  Documentation: https://github.com/DocumentDrivenDX/ddx
  Issues & Support: https://github.com/DocumentDrivenDX/ddx/issues

Usage:
  ddx [command]

Available Commands:
  agent       Invoke AI agents with harness dispatch, quorum, and session logging
  bead        Manage beads (portable work items)
  checkpoint  Create, list, or restore named git checkpoints
  completion  Generate completion script
  config      Configure DDx settings
  doc         Document dependency graph and staleness tracking
  doctor      Check installation health and diagnose issues
  exec        Validate and run DDx execution definitions
  help        Help about any command
  init        Initialize DDx in current project
  install     Install a package or resource
  installed   List installed packages
  jq          Process JSON with jq expressions
  list        List available DDx resources
  log         Show DDX asset history
  metric      Validate and run metric definitions
  metrics     Show process metrics derived from beads and agent sessions
  outdated    List installed packages with available updates
  persona     Manage AI personas for consistent interactions
  prompts     Manage AI prompts
  search      Search available packages
  server      Start the DDx HTTP and MCP server
  skills      Validate bundled skill packages
  status      Show version and status information
  uninstall   Remove an installed package
  update      Update DDx CLI or installed plugins
  upgrade     Upgrade DDx to the latest version
  verify      Verify installed package integrity
  version     Show version information
  work        Work the bead execution queue

Flags:
      --config string              config file (default is $HOME/.ddx.yml)
  -h, --help                       help for ddx
      --library-base-path string   override path for DDx library location
  -v, --verbose                    verbose output

The unwired state is a process failure (the implementer wrote and tested the code but didn't replace the old code path). Wiring is the right fix; delete only if the underlying behavior is genuinely obsolete (none of these are).

  cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_conflict_recovery.go:118 defines RunConflictRecovery(ctx, ConflictRecoveryInput) ConflictRecoveryOutput — the C4 extraction already landed. Status of each AC against current HEAD (97b75512):

1. "Ladder type + Next() method in cli/internal/agent/escalation/"
   PRESENT: cli/internal/agent/escalation/ladder.go defines:
     - type Ladder
     - func NewLadder([]agentlib.ModelInfo) *Ladder
     - func (l *Ladder) Next(actualPower int) (int, error)
     - func (l *Ladder) Tiers() []int
   No vendor names appear; ladder reasons over Power tiers only.

2. "Unit tests cover all transitions"
   PRESENT: cli/internal/agent/escalation/ladder_test.go covers:
     - Tiers distinct/ascending and ignoring unrated (Power<=0) models
     - Next stepping through all-viable tiers, including strictly-greater
     - Exhaustion (ErrLadderExhausted) on top tier and empty/nil ladders
     - Skip-tier returning *NoViableProviderError, then bumping past it
     - All-non-viable catalog exhausting via repeated skips
     - Partial availability (Available xor AutoRoutable) counted non-viable

3. "Skip-tier returns typed NoViableProviderError"
   PRESENT: ladder.go declares `type NoViableProviderError struct{ Floor int }`
   with Error() method; Next returns &NoViableProviderError{Floor: tier}
   when the next tier above actualPower has no viable model.
   TestLadder_Next_SkipTierReturnsNoViableProviderError uses errors.As to
   confirm the typed error and Floor value.

4. "Tests assert ladder uses RoutingActual.Power as input for next-floor
   computation"
   PRESENT: TestLadder_Next_UsesRoutingActualPower constructs a stand-in
   RoutingActual record, passes prev.Power directly to Next, asserts the
   returned floor (90 from prev.Power=70), and additionally swaps
   Provider/Model/Harness to confirm Power is the sole signal.

  $ cd cli && go test ./internal/agent/escalation/
  ok  	github.com/DocumentDrivenDX/ddx/internal/agent/escalation	0.002s

2. Implementation reads DIFF_BASE/DIFF_HEAD/PROJECT_ROOT/EVIDENCE_DIR from env; computes new symbols from diff; runs deadcode-rooted reachability; writes result.json conforming to REACH-PROTO schema.
3. // wiring:pending <bead-id> annotation parsed; bead existence + open-status verified against .ddx/beads.jsonl; invalid annotation = violation.
4. _test.go files excluded from both root selection and reachability calculation (so a unit-test-only reference does NOT satisfy the check).
5. Self-test: synthetic project with one unwired exported function returns status=block with that symbol in violations.
6. Self-test: same project with the function wired into cmd handler returns status=pass.
7. Self-test: same project with // wiring:pending annotation citing an open bead returns status=pass; annotation citing a closed/missing bead returns status=block.
8. This project installs it: .ddx/checks/production-reachability.yaml committed; ddx ac run on any current bead succeeds (or BLOCKs with the known unwired functions — see REACH-BACKFILL for resolution).
9. cd cli && go test green; lefthook pre-commit passes.

Evidence file at .ddx/executions/<run-id>/production-reachability.json shows the check ran and its findings.","claimed-at":"2026-05-03T12:30:48Z","claimed-machine":"eitri","claimed-pid":"463338","closing_commit_sha":"3e58316310e9cf2122afed341ec0ca0a63b6f66d","created_at":"2026-05-02T20:35:10.781525398Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ddx-ea76e1b8","depends_on_id":"ddx-a946c744","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-05-02T20:35:13Z"}],"description":"Ship the Go-specific production-reachability check as a library resource conforming to the REACH-PROTO check protocol. NOT installed by default for arbitrary projects (Go-opinion would leak through ddx init). This project (ddx itself) opts in by adding .ddx/checks/production-reachability.yaml.

DESIGN
- Location: library/checks/go-production-reachability/ (alongside library/personas/, library/patterns/)
- Implementation: a small Go binary or shell script invoking golang.org/x/tools/cmd/deadcode rooted at configurable entry packages.
- Default entry roots for ddx self-use: cli/cmd/..., cli/internal/server/graphql/..., cli/internal/server/http/...
- Logic:
  1. Read DIFF_BASE..DIFF_HEAD from env. Compute set of new Go symbols (functions, methods, types) added by the diff via AST parse of changed files.
  2. Run deadcode (or callgraph) rooted at the configured entry packages, EXCLUDING _test.go from roots and from reachability.
  3. For each new symbol not reachable from any entry root → violation { file, line, symbol, kind: "unreachable", detail: "no path from entry roots: <list>" }
  4. Recognize // wiring:pending <bead-id> annotation as escape hatch for individual symbols. The check verifies the named bead exists and is open via local beads.jsonl read; otherwise the annotation is rejected.
  5. Write ${EVIDENCE_DIR}/production-reachability.json with the protocol-defined schema.
- Config: library/checks/go-production-reachability/check.yaml template that operators copy to .ddx/checks/ and customize entry_roots for their project.

DOGFOOD
- This project installs it: write .ddx/checks/production-reachability.yaml referencing the library check + cli's entry roots.
- The next ddx try on any bead touching cli/**/*.go will exercise the check.

NOT IN SCOPE
- Auto-install during ddx init (would leak Go-opinion). Operators opt in manually or via ddx install check (future, separate bead).
- Coverage-based "exercised via CLI" check (entered_via flavor). Static reachability is sufficient for the immediate problem.
- Python/TS/Rust analogues. Future contributions; protocol supports them.

.ddx/executions/20260503T124415-1a86a675/prompt.md:102:+    "description": "Ship the Go-specific production-reachability check as a library resource conforming to the REACH-PROTO check protocol. NOT installed by default for arbitrary projects (Go-opinion would leak through ddx init). This project (ddx itself) opts in by adding .ddx/checks/production-reachability.yaml.

DESIGN
- Location: library/checks/go-production-reachability/ (alongside library/personas/, library/patterns/)
- Implementation: a small Go binary or shell script invoking golang.org/x/tools/cmd/deadcode rooted at configurable entry packages.
- Default entry roots for ddx self-use: cli/cmd/..., cli/internal/server/graphql/..., cli/internal/server/http/...
- Logic:
  1. Read DIFF_BASE..DIFF_HEAD from env. Compute set of new Go symbols (functions, methods, types) added by the diff via AST parse of changed files.
  2. Run deadcode (or callgraph) rooted at the configured entry packages, EXCLUDING _test.go from roots and from reachability.
  3. For each new symbol not reachable from any entry root → violation { file, line, symbol, kind: "unreachable", detail: "no path from entry roots: <list>" }
  4. Recognize // wiring:pending <bead-id> annotation as escape hatch for individual symbols. The check verifies the named bead exists and is open via local beads.jsonl read; otherwise the annotation is rejected.
  5. Write ${EVIDENCE_DIR}/production-reachability.json with the protocol-defined schema.
- Config: library/checks/go-production-reachability/check.yaml template that operators copy to .ddx/checks/ and customize entry_roots for their project.

DOGFOOD
- This project installs it: write .ddx/checks/production-reachability.yaml referencing the library check + cli's entry roots.
- The next ddx try on any bead touching cli/**/*.go will exercise the check.

NOT IN SCOPE
- Auto-install during ddx init (would leak Go-opinion). Operators opt in manually or via ddx install check (future, separate bead).
- Coverage-based "exercised via CLI" check (entered_via flavor). Static reachability is sufficient for the immediate problem.
- Python/TS/Rust analogues. Future contributions; protocol supports them.

DEP: REACH-PROTO (above)",

3. // wiring:pending <bead-id> annotation parsed; bead existence + open-status verified against .ddx/beads.jsonl; invalid annotation = violation.
4. _test.go files excluded from both root selection and reachability calculation (so a unit-test-only reference does NOT satisfy the check).

6. Self-test: same project with the function wired into cmd handler returns status=pass.
7. Self-test: same project with // wiring:pending annotation citing an open bead returns status=pass; annotation citing a closed/missing bead returns status=block.
8. This project installs it: .ddx/checks/production-reachability.yaml committed; ddx ac run on any current bead succeeds (or BLOCKs with the known unwired functions — see REACH-BACKFILL for resolution).

.ddx/executions/20260504T021759-c712d0e8/manifest.json:13:    "description": "The bug at ddx-29058e2a (5 CLI flags silently dropped on the server path) is a symptom of a deeper architectural issue: the ExecuteLoopSpec is defined as parallel structs that must be hand-maintained in lockstep. Adding any flag requires editing 5+ places, and forgetting one is silent.

CURRENT STATE (the smell)

Each flag traverses 6 boundaries:

1. cobra extract: cmd.Flags().GetX("flag") → local var (cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go:1573-1611)
2. positional param: passed to executeLoopWithServer / runExecuteLoopLocal (cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go:1627, ~1941 signature)
3. loose JSON map: workerSpec map[string]interface{} (cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go:~1948)
4. server request struct: hand-maintained in handleStartExecuteLoopWorker (cli/internal/server/server.go:2246-2260)
5. ExecuteLoopWorkerSpec literal: server.go:2289-2303 → cli/internal/server/workers.go:25-49
6. runWorker per-field consumption: cli/internal/server/workers.go ~656

ALSO IN SCOPE — the GraphQL parallel path (per codex review):
- workerDispatchAdapter at cli/internal/server/graphql_adapters.go:62-75, 123-137 has its own request struct AND its own StartExecuteLoop literal — same parallel-struct smell on a different transport. Unification must DELETE this too, otherwise the refactor fixes REST and leaves GraphQL re-broken.

Two of those (steps 4 and 5) are typed but maintained in parallel. The 5 dropped flags (opaque_passthrough, max_cost, request_timeout, min_power, max_power) fell out exactly there. PLUS FromRev is also dropped on the server path (cobra flag at agent_cmd.go:1560 + work.go:58, consumed locally at agent_cmd.go:1753 ExecuteBeadRuntime{FromRev: fromRev}, never reaches the server). The bug fix would be local — add fields in 5 places — but the ROOT CAUSE recurs the next time a flag is added.

DESIGN — single canonical ExecuteLoopSpec struct

Define one struct, used at every layer:

  cobra RunE → parseExecuteLoopSpec(cmd) (ExecuteLoopSpec, DispatchOptions)
                ↓
       (--local) runExecuteLoopInline(spec)
                ↓
       (default) httpSubmitExecuteLoop(spec)  ── JSON ──▶ server.handleStartExecuteLoopWorker
                                                   ↓ (decode INTO THE SAME struct, then Validate + ApplyDefaults)
                                                startExecuteLoop(spec)
                                                   ↓ (persist as spec.json — SAME struct, fully-resolved)
                                                runWorker(spec)

Properties:
- One struct definition. JSON tags drive both wire format and disk persistence.
- Cobra binding is the only per-flag code (irreducible — cobra needs to know each flag).
- --local and server paths consume the same struct; behavior parity is structural, not asserted by tests.
- Adding a flag = (a) one struct field + (b) one cobra binding. Drop is impossible by construction — the JSON tag round-trips through wire and persistence.
- DispatchOptions is a SEPARATE small struct holding control-plane flags (--local, --json) that are NOT execution-spec fields and must NOT be persisted on disk.

CRITICAL CONTRACTS (per codex review)

1. Wire format for time.Duration: Go's default time.Duration JSON marshaling produces nanoseconds, but current code sends poll_interval as a string (agent_cmd.go:1969-1971). The unified spec MUST commit to one format — either custom Duration type with string MarshalJSON, or canonical numeric. Pick string (operator-readable spec.json wins) and add UnmarshalJSON tolerating both.

2. Validate + ApplyDefaults lifecycle:
   - parseExecuteLoopSpec returns spec + DispatchOptions, with cobra defaults applied
   - Spec.Validate() is called BEFORE HTTP submit (client-side guard)
   - server.handleStartExecuteLoopWorker decodes → calls Spec.ApplyDefaults() → Spec.Validate() BEFORE StartExecuteLoop (server cannot trust the wire)
   - GraphQL workerDispatchAdapter goes through THE SAME StartExecuteLoop entry point so Validate + ApplyDefaults are guaranteed
   - spec.json on disk is fully-resolved (no implicit defaults at runWorker time)

3. Forward-compat stance: ddx is single-repo, single-version client/server today. Add SpecVersion int field; server returns 400 with capability list if SpecVersion > supported. Newer-server-older-client: existing fields unchanged, new fields ignored on both sides (the current default). Make this explicit in the AC; do not rely on json's silent unknown-field tolerance as the only contract.

4. Backward-compat for old persisted specs: existing spec.json files in .ddx/workers/ have legacy fields (e.g., min_tier/max_tier in older specs at .ddx/workers/worker-20260422T083934-f77c/spec.json:5-6). UnmarshalJSON must tolerate unknown fields when LOADING from disk (json.Decoder default behavior is fine here; do NOT enable DisallowUnknownFields for disk reads).

LOCATION

Likely cli/internal/agent/spec.go or cli/internal/agent/executeloop/spec.go (a small focused package). Importable from both cli/cmd/ and cli/internal/server/ without import cycles. .ddx/executions/20260504T021759-c712d0e8/prompt.md:5:The bug at ddx-29058e2a (5 CLI flags silently dropped on the server path) is a symptom of a deeper architectural issue: the ExecuteLoopSpec is defined as parallel structs that must be hand-maintained in lockstep. Adding any flag requires editing 5+ places, and forgetting one is silent.

CURRENT STATE (the smell)

Each flag traverses 6 boundaries:

1. cobra extract: cmd.Flags().GetX("flag") → local var (cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go:1573-1611)
2. positional param: passed to executeLoopWithServer / runExecuteLoopLocal (cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go:1627, ~1941 signature)
3. loose JSON map: workerSpec map[string]interface{} (cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go:~1948)
4. server request struct: hand-maintained in handleStartExecuteLoopWorker (cli/internal/server/server.go:2246-2260)
5. ExecuteLoopWorkerSpec literal: server.go:2289-2303 → cli/internal/server/workers.go:25-49
6. runWorker per-field consumption: cli/internal/server/workers.go ~656

ALSO IN SCOPE — the GraphQL parallel path (per codex review):
- workerDispatchAdapter at cli/internal/server/graphql_adapters.go:62-75, 123-137 has its own request struct AND its own StartExecuteLoop literal — same parallel-struct smell on a different transport. Unification must DELETE this too, otherwise the refactor fixes REST and leaves GraphQL re-broken.

Two of those (steps 4 and 5) are typed but maintained in parallel. The 5 dropped flags (opaque_passthrough, max_cost, request_timeout, min_power, max_power) fell out exactly there. PLUS FromRev is also dropped on the server path (cobra flag at agent_cmd.go:1560 + work.go:58, consumed locally at agent_cmd.go:1753 ExecuteBeadRuntime{FromRev: fromRev}, never reaches the server). The bug fix would be local — add fields in 5 places — but the ROOT CAUSE recurs the next time a flag is added.

DESIGN — single canonical ExecuteLoopSpec struct

Define one struct, used at every layer:

  cobra RunE → parseExecuteLoopSpec(cmd) (ExecuteLoopSpec, DispatchOptions)
                ↓
       (--local) runExecuteLoopInline(spec)
                ↓
       (default) httpSubmitExecuteLoop(spec)  ── JSON ──▶ server.handleStartExecuteLoopWorker
                                                   ↓ (decode INTO THE SAME struct, then Validate + ApplyDefaults)
                                                startExecuteLoop(spec)
                                                   ↓ (persist as spec.json — SAME struct, fully-resolved)
                                                runWorker(spec)

Properties:
- One struct definition. JSON tags drive both wire format and disk persistence.
- Cobra binding is the only per-flag code (irreducible — cobra needs to know each flag).
- --local and server paths consume the same struct; behavior parity is structural, not asserted by tests.
- Adding a flag = (a) one struct field + (b) one cobra binding. Drop is impossible by construction — the JSON tag round-trips through wire and persistence.
- DispatchOptions is a SEPARATE small struct holding control-plane flags (--local, --json) that are NOT execution-spec fields and must NOT be persisted on disk.

CRITICAL CONTRACTS (per codex review)

1. Wire format for time.Duration: Go's default time.Duration JSON marshaling produces nanoseconds, but current code sends poll_interval as a string (agent_cmd.go:1969-1971). The unified spec MUST commit to one format — either custom Duration type with string MarshalJSON, or canonical numeric. Pick string (operator-readable spec.json wins) and add UnmarshalJSON tolerating both.

2. Validate + ApplyDefaults lifecycle:
   - parseExecuteLoopSpec returns spec + DispatchOptions, with cobra defaults applied
   - Spec.Validate() is called BEFORE HTTP submit (client-side guard)
   - server.handleStartExecuteLoopWorker decodes → calls Spec.ApplyDefaults() → Spec.Validate() BEFORE StartExecuteLoop (server cannot trust the wire)
   - GraphQL workerDispatchAdapter goes through THE SAME StartExecuteLoop entry point so Validate + ApplyDefaults are guaranteed
   - spec.json on disk is fully-resolved (no implicit defaults at runWorker time)

3. Forward-compat stance: ddx is single-repo, single-version client/server today. Add SpecVersion int field; server returns 400 with capability list if SpecVersion > supported. Newer-server-older-client: existing fields unchanged, new fields ignored on both sides (the current default). Make this explicit in the AC; do not rely on json's silent unknown-field tolerance as the only contract.

4. Backward-compat for old persisted specs: existing spec.json files in .ddx/workers/ have legacy fields (e.g., min_tier/max_tier in older specs at .ddx/workers/worker-20260422T083934-f77c/spec.json:5-6). UnmarshalJSON must tolerate unknown fields when LOADING from disk (json.Decoder default behavior is fine here; do NOT enable DisallowUnknownFields for disk reads).

.ddx/executions/20260503T030952-1063db26/manifest.json:12:    "title": "checks: ship Go production-reachability check (library/checks/go-production-reachability)",
.ddx/executions/20260503T030952-1063db26/manifest.json:13:    "description": "Ship the Go-specific production-reachability check as a library resource conforming to the REACH-PROTO check protocol. NOT installed by default for arbitrary projects (Go-opinion would leak through ddx init). This project (ddx itself) opts in by adding .ddx/checks/production-reachability.yaml.

DESIGN
- Location: library/checks/go-production-reachability/ (alongside library/personas/, library/patterns/)
- Implementation: a small Go binary or shell script invoking golang.org/x/tools/cmd/deadcode rooted at configurable entry packages.
- Default entry roots for ddx self-use: cli/cmd/..., cli/internal/server/graphql/..., cli/internal/server/http/...
- Logic:
  1. Read DIFF_BASE..DIFF_HEAD from env. Compute set of new Go symbols (functions, methods, types) added by the diff via AST parse of changed files.
  2. Run deadcode (or callgraph) rooted at the configured entry packages, EXCLUDING _test.go from roots and from reachability.
  3. For each new symbol not reachable from any entry root → violation { file, line, symbol, kind: "unreachable", detail: "no path from entry roots: <list>" }
  4. Recognize // wiring:pending <bead-id> annotation as escape hatch for individual symbols. The check verifies the named bead exists and is open via local beads.jsonl read; otherwise the annotation is rejected.
  5. Write ${EVIDENCE_DIR}/production-reachability.json with the protocol-defined schema.
- Config: library/checks/go-production-reachability/check.yaml template that operators copy to .ddx/checks/ and customize entry_roots for their project.

DOGFOOD
- This project installs it: write .ddx/checks/production-reachability.yaml referencing the library check + cli's entry roots.
- The next ddx try on any bead touching cli/**/*.go will exercise the check.

NOT IN SCOPE
- Auto-install during ddx init (would leak Go-opinion). Operators opt in manually or via ddx install check (future, separate bead).
- Coverage-based "exercised via CLI" check (entered_via flavor). Static reachability is sufficient for the immediate problem.
- Python/TS/Rust analogues. Future contributions; protocol supports them.

DEP: REACH-PROTO (above)",
.ddx/executions/20260503T030952-1063db26/manifest.json:14:    "acceptance": "1. library/checks/go-production-reachability/ exists with: README.md (usage), check.yaml (template), implementation (binary or script).
2. Implementation reads DIFF_BASE/DIFF_HEAD/PROJECT_ROOT/EVIDENCE_DIR from env; computes new symbols from diff; runs deadcode-rooted reachability; writes result.json conforming to REACH-PROTO schema.
3. // wiring:pending <bead-id> annotation parsed; bead existence + open-status verified against .ddx/beads.jsonl; invalid annotation = violation.
4. _test.go files excluded from both root selection and reachability calculation (so a unit-test-only reference does NOT satisfy the check).
5. Self-test: synthetic project with one unwired exported function returns status=block with that symbol in violations.
6. Self-test: same project with the function wired into cmd handler returns status=pass.
7. Self-test: same project with // wiring:pending annotation citing an open bead returns status=pass; annotation citing a closed/missing bead returns status=block.
8. This project installs it: .ddx/checks/production-reachability.yaml committed; ddx ac run on any current bead succeeds (or BLOCKs with the known unwired functions — see REACH-BACKFILL for resolution).
9. cd cli && go test green; lefthook pre-commit passes.
10. Evidence file at .ddx/executions/<run-id>/production-reachability.json shows the check ran and its findings.",

4. server request struct: hand-maintained in handleStartExecuteLoopWorker (cli/internal/server/server.go:2246-2260)
5. ExecuteLoopWorkerSpec literal: server.go:2289-2303 → cli/internal/server/workers.go:25-49
6. runWorker per-field consumption: cli/internal/server/workers.go ~656

ALSO IN SCOPE — the GraphQL parallel path (per codex review):
- workerDispatchAdapter at cli/internal/server/graphql_adapters.go:62-75, 123-137 has its own request struct AND its own StartExecuteLoop literal — same parallel-struct smell on a different transport. Unification must DELETE this too, otherwise the refactor fixes REST and leaves GraphQL re-broken.

Two of those (steps 4 and 5) are typed but maintained in parallel. The 5 dropped flags (opaque_passthrough, max_cost, request_timeout, min_power, max_power) fell out exactly there. PLUS FromRev is also dropped on the server path (cobra flag at agent_cmd.go:1560 + work.go:58, consumed locally at agent_cmd.go:1753 ExecuteBeadRuntime{FromRev: fromRev}, never reaches the server). The bug fix would be local — add fields in 5 places — but the ROOT CAUSE recurs the next time a flag is added.

DESIGN — single canonical ExecuteLoopSpec struct

Define one struct, used at every layer:

  cobra RunE → parseExecuteLoopSpec(cmd) (ExecuteLoopSpec, DispatchOptions)
                ↓
       (--local) runExecuteLoopInline(spec)
                ↓
       (default) httpSubmitExecuteLoop(spec)  ── JSON ──▶ server.handleStartExecuteLoopWorker
                                                   ↓ (decode INTO THE SAME struct, then Validate + ApplyDefaults)
                                                startExecuteLoop(spec)
                                                   ↓ (persist as spec.json — SAME struct, fully-resolved)
                                                runWorker(spec)

Properties:
- One struct definition. JSON tags drive both wire format and disk persistence.
- Cobra binding is the only per-flag code (irreducible — cobra needs to know each flag).
- --local and server paths consume the same struct; behavior parity is structural, not asserted by tests.
- Adding a flag = (a) one struct field + (b) one cobra binding. Drop is impossible by construction — the JSON tag round-trips through wire and persistence.
- DispatchOptions is a SEPARATE small struct holding control-plane flags (--local, --json) that are NOT execution-spec fields and must NOT be persisted on disk.

CRITICAL CONTRACTS (per codex review)

1. Wire format for time.Duration: Go's default time.Duration JSON marshaling produces nanoseconds, but current code sends poll_interval as a string (agent_cmd.go:1969-1971). The unified spec MUST commit to one format — either custom Duration type with string MarshalJSON, or canonical numeric. Pick string (operator-readable spec.json wins) and add UnmarshalJSON tolerating both.

2. Validate + ApplyDefaults lifecycle:
   - parseExecuteLoopSpec returns spec + DispatchOptions, with cobra defaults applied
   - Spec.Validate() is called BEFORE HTTP submit (client-side guard)
   - server.handleStartExecuteLoopWorker decodes → calls Spec.ApplyDefaults() → Spec.Validate() BEFORE StartExecuteLoop (server cannot trust the wire)
   - GraphQL workerDispatchAdapter goes through THE SAME StartExecuteLoop entry point so Validate + ApplyDefaults are guaranteed
   - spec.json on disk is fully-resolved (no implicit defaults at runWorker time)

3. Forward-compat stance: ddx is single-repo, single-version client/server today. Add SpecVersion int field; server returns 400 with capability list if SpecVersion > supported. Newer-server-older-client: existing fields unchanged, new fields ignored on both sides (the current default). Make this explicit in the AC; do not rely on json's silent unknown-field tolerance as the only contract.

4. Backward-compat for old persisted specs: existing spec.json files in .ddx/workers/ have legacy fields (e.g., min_tier/max_tier in older specs at .ddx/workers/worker-20260422T083934-f77c/spec.json:5-6). UnmarshalJSON must tolerate unknown fields when LOADING from disk (json.Decoder default behavior is fine here; do NOT enable DisallowUnknownFields for disk reads).

LOCATION

Likely cli/internal/agent/spec.go or cli/internal/agent/executeloop/spec.go (a small focused package). Importable from both cli/cmd/ and cli/internal/server/ without import cycles. NOTE: REACH-PROTO (ddx-a946c744) needs to know about this new package as an entry-root for the production-reachability checker.

NOT IN SCOPE

- Other commands (ddx agent run, ddx try, ddx run, MCP agent dispatch, ddx work passthrough variants) — same smell may exist; covered by the sister audit bead ddx-fb290074.
- Reworking client→server transport (HTTP/JSON stays as-is).
- Changing validation logic itself — only ensuring it lives on the spec so both paths apply it.
- Renaming flags or reshaping CLI surface.
- Reshaping LabelFilter from string to []string — that is a picker semantics change, owned by ddx-9d55601f. Keep LabelFilter as string to keep ddx-9d55601f landing in parallel.

USER-OBSERVED SYMPTOM (originally what filed this bead)
- ddx work --harness claude works under --local but fails when going through the server.
- Root cause: opaque_passthrough was dropped at the server's request struct. With OpaquePassthrough lost, the server-spawned worker validates the harness against its provider list (which rejects 'claude') instead of treating it as opaque-passthrough.
- The unification refactor solves this by construction — there's no second struct to omit OpaquePassthrough from. NOTE: OpaquePassthrough has TWO skip points to verify (cli/internal/server/workers.go:314-317 AND :752-773), not one.

ABSORBS THE NARROW FIX
The 5 originally-listed dropped flags + FromRev all become fields on the unified spec. They cannot be dropped after this refactor. The refactor IS the fix.

SEQUENCING WITH IN-FLIGHT BEADS

- ddx-dc157075 (worker stay-alive): touches execute_bead_loop.go's Run loop. Should land BEFORE this OR be tightly coordinated. If unification lands first, dc157075 redoes defaulting + persistence. Recommend dc157075 first.
- ddx-9d55601f (picker priority bug): does NOT block on this. Keep LabelFilter type stable. Picker fix lands in parallel.
- ddx-a946c744 (REACH-PROTO): needs to know about new cli/internal/agent/executeloop/ package as entry root.

.ddx/executions/20260503T025720-bb59ccca/prompt.md:3:    <title>ddx try: pre-merge production-reachability check protocol + runner</title>
.ddx/executions/20260503T025720-bb59ccca/prompt.md:5:Add a language-agnostic check protocol to ddx so projects can declare mechanical pre-merge gates that ddx try evaluates before any worktree is merged back. Solves the recurring "implemented but not wired" failure mode at the platform layer rather than per-project.

CONTEXT
- Audit at /tmp/agent-surface-dead-code-audit.md surfaced 4 functions with tests but no production caller; reviewer didn't catch it because the diff "looked right" and tests passed.
- ac_structural_check.go (cli/internal/agent/) is the existing seed: parses claims from AC prose for test_name/file_deleted/field_removed. It can't catch the unwired case because a unit test naming the function satisfies the claim vacuously.
- Prior plan considered embedding Go-specific call-graph analysis directly in cli/internal/agent/. Rejected as a boundary violation (CLAUDE.md: "DDx provides primitives; HELIX and others provide opinions"). Each language ecosystem has its own dead-code tooling (Go: deadcode/callgraph; Python: vulture; TS: ts-prune/knip; Rust: cargo-udeps).

DESIGN — protocol (declarative, plugin-based)
- Discovery: .ddx/checks/*.yaml each declares one check: { name, command, when: pre_merge, applies_to: { paths: [globs] | always, labels: [...] } }
- Inputs to check command (env vars): BEAD_ID, DIFF_BASE, DIFF_HEAD, PROJECT_ROOT, EVIDENCE_DIR, RUN_ID
- Output contract: check writes ${EVIDENCE_DIR}/${name}.json with schema { status: pass|block|error, message: string, violations: [{file, line, symbol, kind, detail}] }
- Exit code 0 = ran-to-completion (status field is authoritative); non-zero = check itself errored (treated as BLOCK with status=error)
- Hook point: pre_merge — runs in the parent (after worker's worktree work is done, BEFORE the merge-back into integration branch). BLOCK aborts the merge; the worktree stays preserved per existing conventions for human resolution.
- Bypass: bead-level annotation checks_bypass: [{name: <check>, reason: <text>, bead: <bead-id>}] recorded in events. No CLI flag — bypass must be tracked.
- Standalone: ddx ac run <bead-id> [--check <name>] runs the same checks locally so devs can iterate without spawning a full try attempt.

WHY pre_merge (NOT pre_close in the worker)
- User directive: "We can't afford to merge this kind of dead code." Worker-side checks could be bypassed by a worker that ignores BLOCK; parent-side gate cannot.
- Worker still gets feedback via ddx ac run for local iteration.

NOT IN SCOPE
- Multiple lifecycle hook points beyond pre_merge (add later if needed).
- Check dependencies / ordering (independent + parallel until proven insufficient).
- Plugin manifests, capability flags, version negotiation.
- Auto-discovery beyond .ddx/checks/*.yaml.
- Retry / timeout policy (project owns; checks should be fast).
- Any specific language check (separate beads).

OUT OF SCOPE: REACH-GO (the Go-specific deadcode-based check) and REACH-BACKFILL (the systematic application across cli/) are separate beads that depend on this one.

FILES (anticipated)
- cli/internal/checks/protocol.go — config schema + JSON I/O types
- cli/internal/checks/loader.go — read .ddx/checks/*.yaml
- cli/internal/checks/runner.go — subprocess invocation, env injection, output parsing, evidence dir mgmt
- cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_loop.go — wire pre_merge call before merge step; BLOCK aborts merge
- cli/cmd/ac.go — ddx ac run subcommand
- cli/internal/checks/*_test.go — table-driven tests for protocol contract

.ddx/executions/20260504T022245-36a2ca7b/prompt.md:164:+    "description": "The bug at ddx-29058e2a (5 CLI flags silently dropped on the server path) is a symptom of a deeper architectural issue: the ExecuteLoopSpec is defined as parallel structs that must be hand-maintained in lockstep. Adding any flag requires editing 5+ places, and forgetting one is silent.

CURRENT STATE (the smell)

Each flag traverses 6 boundaries:

1. cobra extract: cmd.Flags().GetX("flag") → local var (cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go:1573-1611)
2. positional param: passed to executeLoopWithServer / runExecuteLoopLocal (cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go:1627, ~1941 signature)
3. loose JSON map: workerSpec map[string]interface{} (cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go:~1948)
4. server request struct: hand-maintained in handleStartExecuteLoopWorker (cli/internal/server/server.go:2246-2260)
5. ExecuteLoopWorkerSpec literal: server.go:2289-2303 → cli/internal/server/workers.go:25-49
6. runWorker per-field consumption: cli/internal/server/workers.go ~656

ALSO IN SCOPE — the GraphQL parallel path (per codex review):
- workerDispatchAdapter at cli/internal/server/graphql_adapters.go:62-75, 123-137 has its own request struct AND its own StartExecuteLoop literal — same parallel-struct smell on a different transport. Unification must DELETE this too, otherwise the refactor fixes REST and leaves GraphQL re-broken.

Two of those (steps 4 and 5) are typed but maintained in parallel. The 5 dropped flags (opaque_passthrough, max_cost, request_timeout, min_power, max_power) fell out exactly there. PLUS FromRev is also dropped on the server path (cobra flag at agent_cmd.go:1560 + work.go:58, consumed locally at agent_cmd.go:1753 ExecuteBeadRuntime{FromRev: fromRev}, never reaches the server). The bug fix would be local — add fields in 5 places — but the ROOT CAUSE recurs the next time a flag is added.

DESIGN — single canonical ExecuteLoopSpec struct

Define one struct, used at every layer:

  cobra RunE → parseExecuteLoopSpec(cmd) (ExecuteLoopSpec, DispatchOptions)
                ↓
       (--local) runExecuteLoopInline(spec)
                ↓
       (default) httpSubmitExecuteLoop(spec)  ── JSON ──▶ server.handleStartExecuteLoopWorker
                                                   ↓ (decode INTO THE SAME struct, then Validate + ApplyDefaults)
                                                startExecuteLoop(spec)
                                                   ↓ (persist as spec.json — SAME struct, fully-resolved)
                                                runWorker(spec)

Properties:
- One struct definition. JSON tags drive both wire format and disk persistence.
- Cobra binding is the only per-flag code (irreducible — cobra needs to know each flag).
- --local and server paths consume the same struct; behavior parity is structural, not asserted by tests.
- Adding a flag = (a) one struct field + (b) one cobra binding. Drop is impossible by construction — the JSON tag round-trips through wire and persistence.
- DispatchOptions is a SEPARATE small struct holding control-plane flags (--local, --json) that are NOT execution-spec fields and must NOT be persisted on disk.

CRITICAL CONTRACTS (per codex review)

1. Wire format for time.Duration: Go's default time.Duration JSON marshaling produces nanoseconds, but current code sends poll_interval as a string (agent_cmd.go:1969-1971). The unified spec MUST commit to one format — either custom Duration type with string MarshalJSON, or canonical numeric. Pick string (operator-readable spec.json wins) and add UnmarshalJSON tolerating both.

2. Validate + ApplyDefaults lifecycle:
   - parseExecuteLoopSpec returns spec + DispatchOptions, with cobra defaults applied
   - Spec.Validate() is called BEFORE HTTP submit (client-side guard)
   - server.handleStartExecuteLoopWorker decodes → calls Spec.ApplyDefaults() → Spec.Validate() BEFORE StartExecuteLoop (server cannot trust the wire)
   - GraphQL workerDispatchAdapter goes through THE SAME StartExecuteLoop entry point so Validate + ApplyDefaults are guaranteed
   - spec.json on disk is fully-resolved (no implicit defaults at runWorker time)

3. Forward-compat stance: ddx is single-repo, single-version client/server today. Add SpecVersion int field; server returns 400 with capability list if SpecVersion > supported. Newer-server-older-client: existing fields unchanged, new fields ignored on both sides (the current default). Make this explicit in the AC; do not rely on json's silent unknown-field tolerance as the only contract.

4. Backward-compat for old persisted specs: existing spec.json files in .ddx/workers/ have legacy fields (e.g., min_tier/max_tier in older specs at .ddx/workers/worker-20260422T083934-f77c/spec.json:5-6). UnmarshalJSON must tolerate unknown fields when LOADING from disk (json.Decoder default behavior is fine here; do NOT enable DisallowUnknownFields for disk reads).

NOT IN SCOPE

- Changing validation logic itself — only ensuring it lives on the spec so both paths apply it.
- Renaming flags or reshaping CLI surface.
- Reshaping LabelFilter from string to []string — that is a picker semantics change, owned by ddx-9d55601f. Keep LabelFilter as string to keep ddx-9d55601f landing in parallel.

.ddx/executions/20260503T123048-397071dc/manifest.json:12:    "title": "checks: ship Go production-reachability check (library/checks/go-production-reachability)",
.ddx/executions/20260503T123048-397071dc/manifest.json:13:    "description": "Ship the Go-specific production-reachability check as a library resource conforming to the REACH-PROTO check protocol. NOT installed by default for arbitrary projects (Go-opinion would leak through ddx init). This project (ddx itself) opts in by adding .ddx/checks/production-reachability.yaml.

DESIGN
- Location: library/checks/go-production-reachability/ (alongside library/personas/, library/patterns/)
- Implementation: a small Go binary or shell script invoking golang.org/x/tools/cmd/deadcode rooted at configurable entry packages.
- Default entry roots for ddx self-use: cli/cmd/..., cli/internal/server/graphql/..., cli/internal/server/http/...
- Logic:
  1. Read DIFF_BASE..DIFF_HEAD from env. Compute set of new Go symbols (functions, methods, types) added by the diff via AST parse of changed files.
  2. Run deadcode (or callgraph) rooted at the configured entry packages, EXCLUDING _test.go from roots and from reachability.
  3. For each new symbol not reachable from any entry root → violation { file, line, symbol, kind: "unreachable", detail: "no path from entry roots: <list>" }
  4. Recognize // wiring:pending <bead-id> annotation as escape hatch for individual symbols. The check verifies the named bead exists and is open via local beads.jsonl read; otherwise the annotation is rejected.
  5. Write ${EVIDENCE_DIR}/production-reachability.json with the protocol-defined schema.
- Config: library/checks/go-production-reachability/check.yaml template that operators copy to .ddx/checks/ and customize entry_roots for their project.

DOGFOOD
- This project installs it: write .ddx/checks/production-reachability.yaml referencing the library check + cli's entry roots.
- The next ddx try on any bead touching cli/**/*.go will exercise the check.

NOT IN SCOPE
- Auto-install during ddx init (would leak Go-opinion). Operators opt in manually or via ddx install check (future, separate bead).
- Coverage-based "exercised via CLI" check (entered_via flavor). Static reachability is sufficient for the immediate problem.
- Python/TS/Rust analogues. Future contributions; protocol supports them.

DEP: REACH-PROTO (above)",
.ddx/executions/20260503T123048-397071dc/manifest.json:14:    "acceptance": "1. library/checks/go-production-reachability/ exists with: README.md (usage), check.yaml (template), implementation (binary or script).
2. Implementation reads DIFF_BASE/DIFF_HEAD/PROJECT_ROOT/EVIDENCE_DIR from env; computes new symbols from diff; runs deadcode-rooted reachability; writes result.json conforming to REACH-PROTO schema.
3. // wiring:pending <bead-id> annotation parsed; bead existence + open-status verified against .ddx/beads.jsonl; invalid annotation = violation.
4. _test.go files excluded from both root selection and reachability calculation (so a unit-test-only reference does NOT satisfy the check).
5. Self-test: synthetic project with one unwired exported function returns status=block with that symbol in violations.
6. Self-test: same project with the function wired into cmd handler returns status=pass.
7. Self-test: same project with // wiring:pending annotation citing an open bead returns status=pass; annotation citing a closed/missing bead returns status=block.
8. This project installs it: .ddx/checks/production-reachability.yaml committed; ddx ac run on any current bead succeeds (or BLOCKs with the known unwired functions — see REACH-BACKFILL for resolution).

.ddx/executions/20260503T123048-397071dc/prompt.md:3:    <title>checks: ship Go production-reachability check (library/checks/go-production-reachability)</title>
.ddx/executions/20260503T123048-397071dc/prompt.md:5:Ship the Go-specific production-reachability check as a library resource conforming to the REACH-PROTO check protocol. NOT installed by default for arbitrary projects (Go-opinion would leak through ddx init). This project (ddx itself) opts in by adding .ddx/checks/production-reachability.yaml.

DESIGN
- Location: library/checks/go-production-reachability/ (alongside library/personas/, library/patterns/)
- Implementation: a small Go binary or shell script invoking golang.org/x/tools/cmd/deadcode rooted at configurable entry packages.
- Default entry roots for ddx self-use: cli/cmd/..., cli/internal/server/graphql/..., cli/internal/server/http/...
- Logic:
  1. Read DIFF_BASE..DIFF_HEAD from env. Compute set of new Go symbols (functions, methods, types) added by the diff via AST parse of changed files.
  2. Run deadcode (or callgraph) rooted at the configured entry packages, EXCLUDING _test.go from roots and from reachability.
  3. For each new symbol not reachable from any entry root → violation { file, line, symbol, kind: "unreachable", detail: "no path from entry roots: <list>" }
  4. Recognize // wiring:pending <bead-id> annotation as escape hatch for individual symbols. The check verifies the named bead exists and is open via local beads.jsonl read; otherwise the annotation is rejected.
  5. Write ${EVIDENCE_DIR}/production-reachability.json with the protocol-defined schema.
- Config: library/checks/go-production-reachability/check.yaml template that operators copy to .ddx/checks/ and customize entry_roots for their project.

DOGFOOD
- This project installs it: write .ddx/checks/production-reachability.yaml referencing the library check + cli's entry roots.
- The next ddx try on any bead touching cli/**/*.go will exercise the check.

NOT IN SCOPE
- Auto-install during ddx init (would leak Go-opinion). Operators opt in manually or via ddx install check (future, separate bead).
- Coverage-based "exercised via CLI" check (entered_via flavor). Static reachability is sufficient for the immediate problem.
- Python/TS/Rust analogues. Future contributions; protocol supports them.

DEP: REACH-PROTO (above)
.ddx/executions/20260503T123048-397071dc/prompt.md:8:1. library/checks/go-production-reachability/ exists with: README.md (usage), check.yaml (template), implementation (binary or script).
2. Implementation reads DIFF_BASE/DIFF_HEAD/PROJECT_ROOT/EVIDENCE_DIR from env; computes new symbols from diff; runs deadcode-rooted reachability; writes result.json conforming to REACH-PROTO schema.
3. // wiring:pending <bead-id> annotation parsed; bead existence + open-status verified against .ddx/beads.jsonl; invalid annotation = violation.
4. _test.go files excluded from both root selection and reachability calculation (so a unit-test-only reference does NOT satisfy the check).
5. Self-test: synthetic project with one unwired exported function returns status=block with that symbol in violations.
6. Self-test: same project with the function wired into cmd handler returns status=pass.
7. Self-test: same project with // wiring:pending annotation citing an open bead returns status=pass; annotation citing a closed/missing bead returns status=block.
8. This project installs it: .ddx/checks/production-reachability.yaml committed; ddx ac run on any current bead succeeds (or BLOCKs with the known unwired functions — see REACH-BACKFILL for resolution).
9. cd cli && go test green; lefthook pre-commit passes.
10. Evidence file at .ddx/executions/<run-id>/production-reachability.json shows the check ran and its findings.
.ddx/executions/20260502T204932-0e555908/manifest.json:12:    "title": "ddx try: pre-merge production-reachability check protocol + runner",
.ddx/executions/20260502T204932-0e555908/manifest.json:13:    "description": "Add a language-agnostic check protocol to ddx so projects can declare mechanical pre-merge gates that ddx try evaluates before any worktree is merged back. Solves the recurring "implemented but not wired" failure mode at the platform layer rather than per-project.

CONTEXT
- Audit at /tmp/agent-surface-dead-code-audit.md surfaced 4 functions with tests but no production caller; reviewer didn't catch it because the diff "looked right" and tests passed.
- ac_structural_check.go (cli/internal/agent/) is the existing seed: parses claims from AC prose for test_name/file_deleted/field_removed. It can't catch the unwired case because a unit test naming the function satisfies the claim vacuously.
- Prior plan considered embedding Go-specific call-graph analysis directly in cli/internal/agent/. Rejected as a boundary violation (CLAUDE.md: "DDx provides primitives; HELIX and others provide opinions"). Each language ecosystem has its own dead-code tooling (Go: deadcode/callgraph; Python: vulture; TS: ts-prune/knip; Rust: cargo-udeps).

DESIGN — protocol (declarative, plugin-based)
- Discovery: .ddx/checks/*.yaml each declares one check: { name, command, when: pre_merge, applies_to: { paths: [globs] | always, labels: [...] } }
- Inputs to check command (env vars): BEAD_ID, DIFF_BASE, DIFF_HEAD, PROJECT_ROOT, EVIDENCE_DIR, RUN_ID
- Output contract: check writes ${EVIDENCE_DIR}/${name}.json with schema { status: pass|block|error, message: string, violations: [{file, line, symbol, kind, detail}] }
- Exit code 0 = ran-to-completion (status field is authoritative); non-zero = check itself errored (treated as BLOCK with status=error)
- Hook point: pre_merge — runs in the parent (after worker's worktree work is done, BEFORE the merge-back into integration branch). BLOCK aborts the merge; the worktree stays preserved per existing conventions for human resolution.
- Bypass: bead-level annotation checks_bypass: [{name: <check>, reason: <text>, bead: <bead-id>}] recorded in events. No CLI flag — bypass must be tracked.
- Standalone: ddx ac run <bead-id> [--check <name>] runs the same checks locally so devs can iterate without spawning a full try attempt.

WHY pre_merge (NOT pre_close in the worker)
- User directive: "We can't afford to merge this kind of dead code." Worker-side checks could be bypassed by a worker that ignores BLOCK; parent-side gate cannot.
- Worker still gets feedback via ddx ac run for local iteration.

NOT IN SCOPE
- Multiple lifecycle hook points beyond pre_merge (add later if needed).
- Check dependencies / ordering (independent + parallel until proven insufficient).
- Plugin manifests, capability flags, version negotiation.
- Auto-discovery beyond .ddx/checks/*.yaml.
- Retry / timeout policy (project owns; checks should be fast).
- Any specific language check (separate beads).

OUT OF SCOPE: REACH-GO (the Go-specific deadcode-based check) and REACH-BACKFILL (the systematic application across cli/) are separate beads that depend on this one.

FILES (anticipated)
- cli/internal/checks/protocol.go — config schema + JSON I/O types
- cli/internal/checks/loader.go — read .ddx/checks/*.yaml
- cli/internal/checks/runner.go — subprocess invocation, env injection, output parsing, evidence dir mgmt
- cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_loop.go — wire pre_merge call before merge step; BLOCK aborts merge
- cli/cmd/ac.go — ddx ac run subcommand
- cli/internal/checks/*_test.go — table-driven tests for protocol contract",

Both verification commands are still running. I’m polling them to see whether the delete was enough or whether any other metaprompt symbol still needs wiring or cleanup.

# DDx CLI Makefile

# Build variables
VERSION ?= $(shell git describe --tags --always --dirty)
COMMIT ?= $(shell git rev-parse --short HEAD)
DATE ?= $(shell date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ)
LDFLAGS = -ldflags "-X main.version=${VERSION} -X main.commit=${COMMIT} -X main.date=${DATE}"

# Build directory
BUILD_DIR = build

# Platforms to build for
PLATFORMS = \
	darwin/amd64 \
	darwin/arm64 \
	linux/amd64 \
	linux/arm64 \
	windows/amd64

.PHONY: all build clean test lint deps help obsidian-migrate obsidian-validate obsidian-test obsidian-dry-run obsidian-nav obsidian-revert bead-schema skill-schema gendocs frontend-build frontend-rebuild frontend-verify copy-skills eval-skill bench-graphql

# Default target
all: clean deps test build

# Build SvelteKit frontend (required before Go build for //go:embed).
# Skip rebuild when no source file is newer than the last build output.
FRONTEND_SRC := $(shell find internal/server/frontend/src internal/server/frontend/static -type f 2>/dev/null)
FRONTEND_STAMP := internal/server/frontend/build/index.html

frontend-build: $(FRONTEND_STAMP)

$(FRONTEND_STAMP): $(FRONTEND_SRC) internal/server/frontend/package.json internal/server/frontend/svelte.config.js internal/server/frontend/vite.config.ts
	@echo "Building SvelteKit frontend..."
	cd internal/server/frontend && bun install --frozen-lockfile && bun run build
	@$(MAKE) --no-print-directory frontend-verify

# Force a frontend rebuild regardless of timestamps
frontend-rebuild:
	@echo "Rebuilding SvelteKit frontend (forced)..."
	cd internal/server/frontend && bun install --frozen-lockfile && bun run build
	@$(MAKE) --no-print-directory frontend-verify

# Verify the embedded frontend bundle is internally consistent: every
# /_app/* asset referenced by index.html must exist on disk. Catches the
# class of bug where index.html and the chunk dir get out of sync (partial
# build, file restore, manual edit) and go:embed silently bakes in a
# broken bundle that 404s every JS chunk to the SPA shell.
frontend-verify:
	@echo "Verifying embedded frontend bundle integrity..."
	@cd internal/server/frontend/build && \
	missing=0; \
	for ref in $$(grep -hoE '"/_app/[^"]+"' index.html | tr -d '"' | sort -u); do \
		path=".$$ref"; \
		if [ ! -f "$$path" ]; then \
			echo "  MISSING: $$ref"; \
			missing=$$((missing+1)); \
		fi; \
	done; \
	if [ $$missing -gt 0 ]; then \
		echo "frontend-verify: $$missing referenced asset(s) missing — run 'make frontend-rebuild'"; \
		exit 1; \
	fi; \
	echo "  ok"

# Copy the canonical top-level skills/ddx/ tree into cli/internal/skills/ddx/
# so //go:embed (which cannot traverse upward out of the package) picks it up.
# Runs as a prereq of build/test/dev so contributors never see stale embedded
# skills. rsync --delete keeps the destination in exact sync.
SKILLS_SRC := ../skills/ddx
SKILLS_DST := internal/skills/ddx
copy-skills:
	@if [ ! -d "$(SKILLS_SRC)" ]; then \
		echo "error: $(SKILLS_SRC) not found (run from cli/)"; exit 1; \
	fi
	@rsync -a --delete $(SKILLS_SRC)/ $(SKILLS_DST)/

# Build for current platform
build: copy-skills frontend-build
	@echo "Building DDx CLI..."
	@mkdir -p $(BUILD_DIR)
	go build $(LDFLAGS) -o $(BUILD_DIR)/ddx .

# Build for all platforms
build-all: clean deps copy-skills
	@echo "Building for all platforms..."
	@mkdir -p $(BUILD_DIR)
	@echo "Building current platform..."
	go build $(LDFLAGS) -o $(BUILD_DIR)/ddx .
	@for platform in $(PLATFORMS); do \
		OS=$$(echo $$platform | cut -d'/' -f1); \
		ARCH=$$(echo $$platform | cut -d'/' -f2); \
		echo "Building $$OS/$$ARCH..."; \
		GOOS=$$OS GOARCH=$$ARCH go build $(LDFLAGS) -o $(BUILD_DIR)/ddx-$$OS-$$ARCH .; \
		if [ $$OS = "windows" ]; then \
			mv $(BUILD_DIR)/ddx-$$OS-$$ARCH $(BUILD_DIR)/ddx-$$OS-$$ARCH.exe; \
		fi; \
	done

# Install dependencies
deps:
	@echo "Installing dependencies..."
	go mod download
	go mod tidy

# Run tests
test: copy-skills
	@echo "Running tests..."
	go test -v ./...

# Run the GraphQL perf harness (ddx-9ce6842a). Seeds a 10 × 500 bead fixture
# and records baseline numbers under docs/helix/06-iterate/perf/. Override
# the fixture size or the report date via BENCH_REPORT_DATE.
BENCH_REPORT_DIR ?= $(abspath ../docs/helix/06-iterate/perf)
BENCH_REPORT_DATE ?= $(shell date -u +%Y-%m-%d)
bench-graphql:
	@echo "Running GraphQL perf harness (fixture: 10 × 500 beads = 5,000)..."
	@mkdir -p $(BENCH_REPORT_DIR)
	DDX_BENCH_REPORT_DIR=$(BENCH_REPORT_DIR) \
	DDX_BENCH_REPORT_DATE=$(BENCH_REPORT_DATE) \
	go test ./internal/server/perf/ -run TestGraphQLPerfMatrix_Baseline -v -timeout 5m
	@echo "Baseline report written to $(BENCH_REPORT_DIR)/$(BENCH_REPORT_DATE)-baseline.md"

# Validate the shared bead record schema
bead-schema:
	@echo "Validating bead record schema..."
	go test ./internal/bead -run 'TestBeadRecordSchema' -count=1

# Validate bundled skill metadata
skill-schema:
	@echo "Validating bundled skill metadata..."
	go run . skills check ../skills internal/skills

# Run linter
lint:
	@echo "Running linter..."
	@if command -v golangci-lint >/dev/null 2>&1; then \
		golangci-lint run; \
	else \
		echo "golangci-lint not found, running go vet instead"; \
		go vet ./...; \
	fi
	@echo "Running routinglint (FEAT-006 anti-reintroduction gate)..."
	@go run ./tools/lint/routinglint/cmd/routinglint ./...

# Install locally for development
install: build
	@echo "Installing DDx locally..."
	cp $(BUILD_DIR)/ddx $(HOME)/.local/bin/ddx
	@echo "DDx installed to ~/.local/bin/ddx"

# Clean build artifacts
clean:
	@echo "Cleaning..."
	rm -rf $(BUILD_DIR)

# Run the CLI (for development)
run:
	go run . $(ARGS)

# Format code
fmt:
	go fmt ./...

# Update dependencies
update-deps:
	go get -u all
	go mod tidy

# Create release archives
release: build-all
	@echo "Creating release archives..."
	@cd $(BUILD_DIR) && \
	for file in ddx-*; do \
		if [[ $$file == *.exe ]]; then \
			zip $${file%.*}.zip $$file; \
		else \
			tar -czf $$file.tar.gz $$file; \
		fi; \
	done

# Development server (watches for changes)
dev: copy-skills
	@echo "Starting development mode..."
	@if command -v air >/dev/null 2>&1; then \
		air; \
	else \
		echo "Air not found. Install with: go install github.com/cosmtrek/air@latest"; \
		echo "Falling back to basic run..."; \
		go run . $(ARGS); \
	fi

# Obsidian integration targets
obsidian-migrate: build
	@echo "Migrating HELIX workflow to Obsidian format..."
	./$(BUILD_DIR)/ddx obsidian migrate workflows/helix

obsidian-validate: build
	@echo "Validating Obsidian format in HELIX workflow..."
	./$(BUILD_DIR)/ddx obsidian validate workflows/helix

obsidian-test:
	@echo "Running Obsidian integration tests..."
	go test ./internal/obsidian/... -v
	go test ./internal/obsidian/converter/... -v
	go test ./internal/obsidian/validator/... -v

obsidian-dry-run: build
	@echo "Running Obsidian migration dry-run..."
	./$(BUILD_DIR)/ddx obsidian migrate --dry-run workflows/helix

obsidian-nav: build
	@echo "Generating navigation hub..."
	./$(BUILD_DIR)/ddx obsidian nav workflows/helix

obsidian-revert: build
	@echo "Reverting Obsidian migration..."
	./$(BUILD_DIR)/ddx obsidian revert --strip-frontmatter workflows/helix

# Generate Hugo markdown docs for every CLI command
gendocs:

data.text: `cli/Makefile` confirms the test suite expects `cli/build/ddx`, so I’m building the CLI the normal way before rerunning `go test ./...`. If that binary exists, the acceptance failures should become informative instead of environment-driven.

# Bead Authoring Template

> A bead's description + AC must be sufficient context for a competent
> sub-agent to execute it without hand-curation.
> — Reliability principle **P7** (`docs/helix/06-iterate/reliability-principles.md`)

This document is the canonical template + rubric for creating beads in
this repository. It exists because the 2026-05-03 bead-quality audit
(`.ddx/executions/20260503T155638-bead57f0cb9e/bead-quality-audit-2026-05-03.md`)
found that only **1 of 20** representative open beads scored 8/8 on
the sufficient-sub-agent-prompt rubric. The most common failure modes
were: no specific test names in AC (80 % miss), no `go test` +
lefthook gate in AC (80 % miss), and no root cause with file:line in
description (65 % miss).

---

## 1. The 8-criterion rubric

Every bead must satisfy:

| # | Criterion | Verifier |
|---|-----------|----------|
| (a) | **Title**: one-line scope clarity, imperative, names subsystem + change | "Pagination off-by-one in bead list endpoint" not "Pagination bug" |
| (b) | **Description**: problem + ROOT CAUSE WITH FILE:LINE + proposed fix + non-scope | Root cause cites `cli/path/file.go:42-50` |
| (c) | **AC**: numbered, verifiable, includes specific test names | At least one AC names `TestFoo` or a `go test -run` filter |
| (d) | **AC** includes "wired-in" assertion when introducing new code paths | New function → AC asserts a caller invokes it (production or test) |
| (e) | **AC** includes `go test` command + lefthook gate | Both `cd cli && go test ./...` and `lefthook run pre-commit` named |
| (f) | **Labels**: phase + area + kind + cross-refs | `phase:N`, `area:*`, `kind:*`, plus adr/spec/prevention if applicable |
| (g) | **Parent + Deps**: explicit | Parent set; deps either listed or "no deps" stated |
| (h) | Description reads as **sufficient sub-agent prompt** | A competent agent given only the bead body can pick a file and run tests without asking |

Score 8/8 = execution-ready. Less = retrofit before dispatch.

---

## 2. Template fields

```text
TITLE
  <area>: <imperative one-line scope>
  e.g. "agent: unify ExecuteLoopSpec across cobra/HTTP/server layers"

TYPE
  task | bug | epic | chore

LABELS
  phase:<N>, area:<subsystem>, kind:<category>
  + cross-refs: prevention, adr:<id>, spec:<id>, story:<n>, refactor

PARENT
  <ddx-id> of governing epic / story / spec
  (omit only if this is a root)

DEPS
  - <ddx-id-1>: <one-line why this dep matters>
  - <ddx-id-2>: <one-line why this dep matters>
  (or explicit "No deps.")

SPEC-ID (when applicable)
  --set spec-id=FEAT-NNN  | SD-NNN | TD-NNN | ADR-NNN

DESCRIPTION
  PROBLEM
    <one-paragraph statement of what is broken or missing>

  ROOT CAUSE
    <function/file:line where the defect lives>
    <what the code does today vs what it should do>
    For new features: ROOT CAUSE -> "no existing implementation"
    is OK; replace with CURRENT STATE describing the gap.

  PROPOSED FIX
    <bulleted concrete changes; cite file:line for each>
    - cli/internal/foo/bar.go:120-145  rename Baz -> Quux
    - cli/internal/foo/bar_test.go      add TestQuux_RoundTrip

  NON-SCOPE
    - <thing readers might assume is included but isn't>
    - <thing covered by sister bead ddx-NNNN>

  INTERSECTIONS (optional)
    - <ddx-id>: <how this bead relates to that one>

ACCEPTANCE
  1. <verifiable assertion 1 — names a Test, file path, command, or
     observable artifact>
  2. <verifiable assertion 2>
  3. ...
  N-1. cd cli && go test ./<paths>/... green.
  N. lefthook run pre-commit passes.
```

---

## 3. Examples

### 3.1 Example of a strong bead (audit score 8/8)

`ddx-1e516bc9` — *"fizeau Execute: explicit Harness + empty Model + non-empty Profile/MinPower must route within harness's eligible models"*

Why it scored 8/8:

- **Title (a)** names subsystem (`fizeau Execute`), the precondition (`explicit Harness + empty Model`), and the expected behavior (`route within harness's eligible models`).
- **Description (b)** opens with **OBSERVED** (one paragraph, file:line) → **SCOPE — UPSTREAM (fizeau) ONLY** → **INVESTIGATION FIRST** (with explicit close-as-no-op outcome) → **UPSTREAM CHANGE** (file:line into `service_execute.go:193-224`, `models.yaml:31-60`, `registry.go`) → explicit **NOT IN SCOPE** list naming five excluded paths.
- **AC (c)** names two concrete tests:
  `TestExecute_ExplicitHarnessEmptyModelWithProfile_RoutesWithinHarness` and
  `TestExecute_ExplicitHarnessEmptyModelNoProfile_FailsClearly`.
- **Wired-in (d)**: AC #6 asserts the resolved model surfaces in
  `.ddx/workers/<id>/spec.json`, `.ddx/executions/<run-id>/result.json`, and the
  workers UI — three distinct call-graph endpoints.
- **Test command + lefthook (e)**: AC #9 — `cd cli && go test ./internal/agent/... ./cmd/... green; lefthook pre-commit passes.`
- **Labels (f)**: `phase:2, area:agent, kind:fix, upstream-fizeau, observed-failure`.
- **Parent + Deps (g)**: parent `ddx-e34994e2`; dep `ddx-cfedee8e` with explanation that the ladder must supply MinPower for fizeau to act on.
- **Sufficient (h)**: a sub-agent given only the bead body knows which file in fizeau to read, what the gap looks like, what the negative-test outcome should be, and how to verify the end-to-end fix without asking.

### 3.2 Example of a weak bead (audit score 1/8) — annotated

`ddx-0793ac75` — *"federation: federated GraphQL resolvers + routing-metadata fields"*

Verbatim description:
```
GraphQL resolvers that aggregate across spokes (federatedWorkers,
federatedRuns, federatedDocuments, etc.). Add routing-metadata fields
exposing which spoke owns each result. Write-routing contract: hub
forwards mutations to owning node, never broadcasts (Story 15
dependency).
```

Verbatim AC:
```
1. federated* resolvers in schema.
2. Routing-metadata visible per result.
3. Mutations route to owner; tested.
4. Tests cover happy + degraded paths.
```

Failures, criterion by criterion:

- **(b) FAIL** — no problem statement (what's broken today?), no root cause, no file:line, no non-scope. The reader cannot tell where the schema lives, where resolvers go, or what "routing-metadata" should look like as a Go type.
- **(c) FAIL** — "tested" and "Tests cover happy + degraded paths" name no `Test*` function. Sub-agent has no way to know what to write or where.
- **(d) PARTIAL** — "tested" implies the resolvers are wired, but no production-call-graph assertion (e.g., "schema.graphql Mutation block exposes field X").
- **(e) FAIL** — no `go test` command, no lefthook gate.
- **(h) FAIL** — a sub-agent reading this cold cannot answer: which schema file? which resolver package? what fields on routing-metadata? what's the wire format? Hand-curation by the operator is the only path forward.

**Retrofit shape** (illustrative — not an actual edit):
```
PROBLEM
  Story 14 needs aggregate-across-spokes resolvers. Today the GraphQL
  schema (cli/internal/server/graphql/schema.graphql) exposes only
  per-node Worker/Run/Document queries. Federation requires
  federated* counterparts that fan out to spokes and stitch results.

ROOT CAUSE
  No federated resolver layer exists. cli/internal/server/graphql_resolver.go
  contains only single-node resolvers. Spoke fan-out is in
  cli/internal/server/federation_spoke.go but nothing in the GraphQL
  layer calls it.

PROPOSED FIX
  - schema.graphql: add Federated{Worker,Run,Document} types with
    `ownerSpoke: String!` field.
  - graphql_resolver.go: add federatedWorkers/federatedRuns/
    federatedDocuments resolvers calling federation_spoke.FanOut.
  - graphql_mutation.go: write-routing helper routes mutations to
    owning node via federation_spoke.RouteToOwner.

NON-SCOPE
  - New non-federated resolvers (out: covered by Story 13).
  - Spoke discovery (out: covered by ddx-fe63969f).

ACCEPTANCE
  1. schema.graphql exposes federatedWorkers/federatedRuns/
     federatedDocuments queries with ownerSpoke metadata.
  2. TestFederatedWorkers_FanOutAggregates verifies fan-out + stitch.
  3. TestFederatedMutation_RoutesToOwner verifies write-routing.
  4. TestFederatedWorkers_DegradedSpoke returns partial results +
     warning.
  5. cd cli && go test ./internal/server/... green.
  6. lefthook run pre-commit passes.
```

---

## 4. Authoring checklist (paste into your editor before running `ddx bead create`)

```text
[ ] (a) Title: imperative, one line, names subsystem + change
[ ] (b) Description has all four sub-elements:
        [ ] PROBLEM (one paragraph)
        [ ] ROOT CAUSE with file:line citation (or CURRENT STATE for new features)
        [ ] PROPOSED FIX (bulleted, file:line per item)
        [ ] NON-SCOPE
[ ] (c) Each AC line is verifiable; at least one names Test* or `go test -run`
[ ] (d) New code path → AC asserts a production or test caller invokes it
[ ] (e) AC includes:
        [ ] cd cli && go test ./<paths>/... green
        [ ] lefthook run pre-commit passes
[ ] (f) Labels: phase:<N>, area:<subsystem>, kind:<category>, +cross-refs
[ ] (g) Parent set; deps listed or "no deps" stated
[ ] (h) Self-test: re-read the bead from cold. Could a competent
        sub-agent execute it without asking a question? If no, retrofit.
[ ] No /tmp/ paths cited as load-bearing context. Inline the relevant
    excerpt instead — /tmp does not survive between machines.
```

---

## 5. Anti-patterns surfaced by the audit

- **`/tmp/` references as load-bearing context.** 5 of 20 audited beads cite a `/tmp/...` plan file. These are functionally broken links to a sub-agent on a different machine. **Inline the excerpt.**
- **"See parent" without inline summary.** Refactor children of `ddx-5cb6e6cd` (C4/C7/C11/C13) systematically defer detail to the parent epic. Each child fails the stand-alone test. **Inline the parent's relevant 5-10 lines.**
- **Vague test phrases.** "Tests cover X." "All execute-bead tests still pass." Both omit the actual `Test*` name or `-run` filter the executor needs.
- **Story-doc copy-paste.** One-paragraph beads (`ddx-0793ac75`, `ddx-0e5c3005`, `ddx-1d52a30c`, `ddx-0ca4ffda`) are story excerpts that were never expanded for execution. Story documents are *upstream* of beads, not the bead itself.
- **Implicit lefthook.** Lefthook runs in pre-commit hooks anyway, but if AC doesn't name it, executors omit it from their own verification before committing.

---

## 6. When to skip a criterion
